title: L10 Volatility
body:
Volatility  Standard volatility measurement • Define σn as the volatility per day between day n-1 and day n, as estimated at end of day n-1 • Define 𝑆𝑖 as the value of market variable at end of day i 𝑆𝑖 • Define 𝑢𝑖 = ln Now, we have: 𝑆𝑖−1 • 2 𝜎𝑛 = σ𝑚 𝑖=1 𝑢𝑛−𝑖 − 𝑢ത 2 𝑚−1 • 𝑢ത = σ𝑚 𝑢 𝑚 𝑖=1 𝑛−𝑖   𝑆𝑖 • Let 𝑢𝑖 = ln 𝑆𝑖−1

• Now we make two simplifications: Simplification • Assume that 𝑢ത = 0 • Replace 𝑚 − 1 by 𝑚 1 𝑚 • This then gives:𝜎𝑛2 = σ𝑖=1 𝑢𝑛−𝑖 2 𝑚  Now to weight things

• So far we have assumed that observations are equally weighted, yielding σ𝑚𝑖=1 𝑢 𝑛−𝑖 𝑚 • But, we can weight observations. 𝑚 This yields: 𝜎𝑛2 = ෍ 𝛼𝑖 𝑢𝑛−𝑖 𝑊ℎ𝑒𝑟𝑒 𝑚

1 = ෍ 𝛼𝑖  Exponentially Weighted Moving Average • In an exponentially weighted moving 2average model, the weights assigned to the 𝑢 decline exponentially as we move back through time • This leads to: 2 2 𝜎𝑛2 = 𝜆𝜎𝑛−1 + 1 − 𝜆 𝑢𝑛−1 • Why do we use this? • Little data needs to be stored • Computationally easy • Allows a decay over time • Can be done recursively • Riskmetrics assumes that 𝜆 = 0.94  ARCH(m)

• Now suppose we have a view about the ‘long run’ variance. • We might hypothesize that the variance on day 𝑛 should be a combination of that long run variance and the other observed deviations over time… • This gives us: 𝑚 𝜎𝑛2 = 𝛾𝑉𝐿 + ෍ 𝛼𝑖 𝑢𝑛−𝑖 𝑊ℎ𝑒𝑟𝑒 𝑚

1 = 𝛾 + ෍ 𝛼𝑖  GARCH (p,q) • In GARCH we assign some weight to the long- run average variance rate • We have 𝑝 numbers of 𝑢 terms and 𝑞 numbers of 𝜎 2 terms

2 2 𝜎𝑛2 = 𝛾𝑉𝐿 + ෍ 𝛼𝑖 𝑢𝑛−𝑖 + ෍ 𝛽𝑖 𝜎𝑛−𝑖 𝑖=1 𝑗=1 𝑊ℎ𝑒𝑟𝑒 1 = 𝛾 + ෍ 𝛼𝑖 + ෍ 𝛽𝑖 𝑖=1 𝑗=1 • We can also express the long run variance as • 𝛾𝑉𝐿 = 𝜔 𝜔 • Where, VL = 1−σ𝑖=1 𝛼𝑖 +σ𝑗=1 𝛽𝑖  How do we get these coefficients?

• The concern is what the coefficient numbers should be • We start with the idea that we want the coefficients to be such that we maximize the likelihood of our estimate being ‘correct’ • Solver will help us do this  Example: a binary event • We are running a casino. We want a rigged game where the customer only wins one in 10 times. • What is our estimate of the proportion of the time, p, that it happens? • The probability of the event happening on one particular trial and not on the others is: 𝑝𝐴 1 − 𝑝𝐴 9 • We can then solve for the probability value that would maximize this – this cross-validates that we want it to payout only 10% of the time estimator here? • Let’s start with the normal distribution with mean of zero (which is what we are using). Denote 𝑣 as the variance. The PDF gives us 1 𝑢2 𝑒 −2𝑣 𝑢2 𝑖 𝑚 1 − • We therefore want to maximize 𝑖=1 ς 𝑒 2𝑣 • We can now take logorithms and rearrange to find that we want to 𝑚 𝑢2 maximize σ𝑖=1 − ln 𝑣 − 𝑣 estimator here? Derivation 𝑢2 𝑖 𝑚 1 − • We have 𝑖=1 ς 𝑒 2𝑣 𝑢2 𝑖 • If we take logs, we get ln 𝑚 ς𝑖=1 𝑒 − 2𝑣

• But, we know that ln 𝑎 × 𝑏 = ln 𝑎 + ln 𝑏 𝑢2 𝑖 • Therefore, we get σ𝑚 𝑖=1 ln 𝑒 − 2𝑣 estimator here? Derivation 𝑢2 𝑖 • Now, we can focus on ln 𝑒 − 2𝑣

• Applying that same rule that ln 𝑎 × 𝑏 = ln 𝑎 + ln 𝑏 , we obtain 𝑢2 𝑖 ln + ln 𝑒 − 2𝑣 𝑐 • Now, we know that ln = ln 𝑐 − ln 𝑑 and that ln 𝑒 𝑥 = 𝑥. 𝑑 𝑢𝑖2 Therefore, we obtain ln 1 − ln 2𝜋𝑣 + − 2𝑣 estimator here? Derivation • Now, we know that ln 21 = 0 and that ln 𝑥 𝑦 = yln 𝑥 . Therefore, we get 1 𝑢𝑖 − ln 2𝜋𝑣 + − 2 2𝑣 1 1 1 𝑢𝑖2 • We can then rearrange to obtain: − ln 2 − ln 𝜋 − ln 𝑣 − 2 2 2 2𝑣 • Now we should note that maximizing 𝑥 is the same as simply maximizing 𝑥. Furthermore, when maximizing, an unchanging scalar can be ignored. Therefore, we can ignore ln 2 and ln 𝜋 . 𝑢𝑖2 • This leaves us with maximizing across − ln 𝑣 − 𝑣 • Therefore, we maximize: 𝑚 𝑢2 ෍ − ln 𝑣 − 𝑣  How do we apply this? Solver! • Start with trial values of ω, α, and β • Calculate the initial values for 𝜎 𝑢2 • Calculate the value of − ln 𝑣 − for each observation 𝑣 𝑚 𝑢2 • Calculate the summation σ𝑖=1 − ln 𝑣 − 𝑣 • Maximize that value by changing 𝜔, 𝛼, and 𝛽 subject to the constraint that 𝜔 + 𝛼 + 𝛽 = 1  Forecasting volatility

• So far, we have looked one period ahead • We can look multiple periods ahead 𝐸 𝜎𝑛+𝑘 = 𝑉𝐿 + 𝛼 + 𝛽 𝑘 𝜎𝑛2 − 𝑉𝐿 • Therefore, the variance for an option expiring in 𝑚 days is 𝑚−1 1 2 ෍ 𝐸 𝜎𝑛+𝑘 𝑚 𝑘=0  What this means for option pricing • If we want to obtain an option price, we can forecast the relevant volatility using the prior formula • That volatility then becomes the input for our BSOPM (or our binomial model as up steps are 𝑢 = 𝑒 𝜎 𝑡 and down steps are 𝑑 = 𝑒 −𝜎 𝑡 )  This logic also applies to correlations • For example, for an equally weighted moving average covariance, we have: 𝑐𝑜𝑣𝑛 = 𝜆𝑐𝑜𝑣𝑛−1 + 1 − 𝜆 𝑥𝑛−1 𝑦𝑛−1   Thanks everyone for attending the course!
